skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X with posting, engagement, and user management via inference.sh CLI. Apps: x/post-tweet, x/post-create (with media), x/post-like, x/post-retweet, x/dm-send, x/user-follow. Capabilities: post tweets, schedule content, like posts, retweet, send DMs, follow users, get profiles. Use for: social media automation, content scheduling, engagement bots, audience growth, X API. Triggers: twitter api, x api, tweet automation, post to twitter, twitter bot, social media automation, x automation, tweet scheduler, twitter integration, post tweet, twitter post, x post, send tweet
skill.summary: Automate Twitter/X posting, engagement, and user management via belt CLI tool
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Security evaluation confirms this is legitimate Twitter/X automation skill documentation. Static analyzer flagged 46 potential issues, but manual review determined all findings are false positives or documented CLI usage patterns. The skill documents belt CLI invocations with hardcoded arguments only. No command injection vectors exist. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags were misidentified table elements and app name fragments. No actual security vulnerabilities present.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Misidentified Cryptographic Pattern (SKILL.md:3)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'x/post-create' as weak crypto algorithm. This is an app ID in the skill description, not cryptographic code. No actual crypto implementation exists in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text 'x/post-create' is an app identifier in YAML frontmatter. Manual inspection confirms no cryptographic algorithms are present.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Misidentified Cryptographic Pattern (SKILL.md:29)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged table header row as weak crypto. This is markdown table formatting, not code. No actual crypto implementation exists in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines 29-31 contain markdown table headers (App, App ID, Description) for listing available commands. No cryptographic code present.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 37 instances of external commands. These are documented belt CLI invocations in SKILL.md, not executable code. All arguments are hardcoded strings with no user input injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill uses allowed-tools: Bash(belt *) - meaning it uses belt CLI for all Twitter API operations. The SKILL.md contains documentation examples, not shell execution code. No injection vectors exist.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs (Informational)
low_findings.description: 7 URLs to inference.sh documentation are hardcoded. These are legitimate documentation links, not security concerns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs reference official inference.sh documentation and documentation pages. Standard documentation practice with no security implications.

content.user_title: Automate Twitter/X with the belt CLI tool
content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X manually takes hours each day. This skill automates posting, engagement, and user interactions through a simple CLI interface that connects to the X API.
actual_capabilities: Post text tweets and tweets with media attachments
Like and retweet posts by ID
Follow and unfollow users by username
Retrieve user profiles and tweet details
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and belt CLI installation
Cannot perform actions requiring elevated permissions without proper X API access
Rate limits depend on your X API tier and authentication setup

use_cases.title: Schedule social media content
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can schedule tweets with images by generating content with AI and posting through the CLI. Works well for product announcements and campaign posts.

use_cases.title: Automate engagement workflows
use_cases.description: Community managers can automate liking and retweeting specific content, or send welcome DMs to new followers based on their username.

use_cases.title: Build content pipelines
use_cases.description: Creators can combine AI image and video generation skills with Twitter posting to create automated content pipelines that generate and publish social media posts.

prompt_templates.title: Post a simple tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to post a tweet saying "{tweet_text}" to my Twitter account.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to share an update or announcement

prompt_templates.title: Post tweet with image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to post a tweet with image. The text should be "{tweet_text}" and the media URL is {image_url}.

prompt_templates.title: Engage with a tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to like the tweet at {tweet_url} and then follow its author.

prompt_templates.title: AI content pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image using AI with the prompt "{image_prompt}", then post it to Twitter with the caption "{caption}" using the twitter-automation skill.
prompt_templates.scenario: For automated content creation and posting

output_examples.input: Post a tweet announcing our new feature launch
output_examples.output: Successfully posted: "Excited to announce our new feature! Check it out at example.com/features". Tweet ID: 1234567890. The tweet is now visible on your profile and will appear in followers' timelines.

output_examples.input: Like and follow the author of tweet ID 9876543210
output_examples.output: Liked tweet ID 9876543210. Followed user @example_user. Engagement actions completed successfully.

best_practices: Always authenticate with belt login before running any Twitter commands
Test commands with sample data using belt app sample before full automation
Monitor X API rate limits and implement delays between bulk operations
anti_patterns: Do not post the same content repeatedly without variation to avoid spam detection
Do not attempt actions beyond your API permissions level
Do not store API credentials in skill documentation or comments

faq.question: How do I set up the twitter-automation skill?
faq.answer: Install the belt CLI with npx skills add belt-sh/cli, run belt login to authenticate with inference.sh, and set up your X API credentials in the inference.sh dashboard.

faq.question: What X API permissions do I need?
faq.answer: You need read and write permissions to post, like, retweet, and send DMs. User follow requires write permissions. Profile reading requires read permissions.

faq.question: Can I schedule tweets for later?
faq.answer: This skill posts tweets immediately. For scheduling, combine it with a task scheduler or cron job, or use external scheduling tools with the belt CLI.

faq.question: What happens if my tweet fails?
faq.answer: The belt CLI will return an error message with the failure reason. Common issues include rate limit exceeded, invalid media URL, or insufficient API permissions.

faq.question: How do I add images or videos to my tweets?
faq.answer: Use the x/post-create app and include a media_url field pointing to a publicly accessible image or video URL. The AI image generation skill can create images you can use.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with my main Twitter account?
faq.answer: Yes, but use caution with automation frequency. Twitter may flag accounts for excessive automated activity. Start with small volumes and monitor your account status.
